title: Feed-forward network
description: The position-wise dense transform that refines each token's hidden vector after attention in a transformer block.
openingSummary: A feed-forward network is the per-token MLP that follows attention in each transformer block, refining each position's hidden state without mixing across the sequence.

whatItIs.title: What It Is
whatItIs.body: A feed-forward network, often shortened to FFN, is the per-token transform that runs after attention inside a transformer block. Attention mixes information across positions first. The FFN then takes one token's updated hidden vector, applies learned nonlinear compute to that one vector, and writes the result back into the residual stream.

whatItOptimizes.title: What It Optimizes
whatItOptimizes.body: The FFN slot adds nonlinear capacity after attention has already gathered context. That helps the model turn mixed context into sharper features, patterns, and intermediate abstractions without paying for another token-to-token interaction step.

practicalBenefit.title: Practical Benefit
practicalBenefit.body: Once you recognize the FFN slot, many paper claims become easier to decode. Standard FFN, SwiGLU, and mixture of experts all live in the same place in the block, so a reader can compare them as alternative ways to spend per-token compute.

howItWorks.title: How It Works
howItWorks.body: The token leaves attention and enters one FFN design. In the dense baseline, every token uses one shared expand-activate-project path. In SwiGLU, that path splits into value and gate branches before recombining. In MoE, a router chooses only a few expert MLPs for that token. The slot stays the same even when the internal path changes.

mathOrComputeSchema.title: Math Or Compute Schema
mathOrComputeSchema.body: These formulas show a dense FFN baseline and a common gated variant. Both act on one token state at a time; the difference is whether the wide hidden path stays single-branch or becomes value-plus-gate.

comparedToNearbyModules.title: Compared To Nearby Modules
comparedToNearbyModules.body: Nearby FFN variants trade dense simplicity, gated expressiveness, and sparse routing. The table below keeps those tradeoffs in one place.

exampleArchitectures.title: Example Architectures
exampleArchitectures.body: Decoder transformers, encoder stacks, and encoder-decoder systems all include an FFN-style block after attention, even when the exact FFN variant changes.

limitationsAndTradeoffs.title: Limitations And Tradeoffs
limitationsAndTradeoffs.body: The FFN does not mix information across tokens by itself, so it depends on attention to bring the right context into each token state first. Different FFN variants also change memory use, routing complexity, and training behavior in different ways.

whyItStillMatters.title: Why It Still Matters
whyItStillMatters.body: Many model changes that sound exotic are really swaps inside this one familiar slot. Understanding the FFN family helps a reader map new papers back to a stable block-level pattern instead of treating every variant as a brand-new architecture.

computeFlow.alt: Feed-forward network comparison showing standard FFN, SwiGLU, and mixture-of-experts variants in the same transformer slot
computeFlow.caption: Switch between dense, gated, and sparse FFN forms to see what changes inside the same post-attention slot

comparisonTable.caption: How the main FFN-family variants compare

standard.pathShape: One dense expand -> activate -> project path
standard.activeCompute: All hidden units in one shared MLP
standard.mainStrength: Simple dense baseline with predictable behavior

swiglu.pathShape: Value branch times SiLU gate branch
swiglu.activeCompute: All hidden units plus a learned gate
swiglu.mainStrength: More expressive feature selection inside the same slot

moe.pathShape: Router selects a few expert MLPs, then merges them
moe.activeCompute: Only top-k experts activate for each token
moe.mainStrength: Adds parameter capacity without activating every expert

ht.definition: Hidden state for token position t.

w1.definition: Projection from model width into the wide hidden layer.

w2.definition: Projection from the wide hidden layer back to model width.

sigma.definition: Pointwise activation applied inside the dense hidden path.

ht.definition: Hidden state for token position t.

wv.definition: Projection that builds the value branch.

wg.definition: Projection that builds the gate branch.

wo.definition: Projection that maps the gated hidden state back to model width.

odot.definition: Elementwise multiplication between the value and gate branches.

sparseNote.label: Sparse path: router sends each token to top-k experts
